hypothesis: Applying class-balancing strategies during training—specifically oversampling rare reaction templates and undersampling frequent ones to achieve a more uniform template frequency distribution—will disproportionately improve top-k accuracy for rare templates (frequency 1–10) without substantially degrading accuracy for common templates (frequency >10,000), thereby reducing the performance disparity between rare and common templates by at least 30% for template-free models such as RootAligned, while simultaneously improving out-of-distribution generalization on the Pistachio OOD test set beyond the 1–2% top-10 accuracy ceiling observed with standard training.
